title: Platform Summary
text: Cascade is Anvil Energy's battery-management-system platform, deployed since 2018 across electric-vehicle packs ranging from compact urban delivery vans to full-size passenger sedans built by several regional automakers under license. At its core, Cascade combines a distributed cell-voltage monitoring architecture with a centralized pack controller that fuses per-cell measurements into a single state-of-charge and state-of-health estimate for the whole pack. Rather than measuring only pack-level voltage and current, Cascade monitors every individual cell continuously, letting it detect a single weak or degrading cell long before that cell's behavior would be visible at the pack terminals. This cell-level visibility is also what makes Cascade's balancing and thermal-runaway-detection features possible, since both depend on comparing one cell's behavior against its neighbors rather than against a pack-wide average that could mask an individual outlier.

title: Cell-Voltage Monitoring Hardware
text: Cascade's cell-voltage monitoring IC, the CVM-16, monitors up to 16 series-connected cells per device and reports each cell's voltage through a 16-bit delta-sigma ADC with 44-microvolt resolution. A full 16-cell scan completes in under 0.9 milliseconds, and up to 12 CVM-16 devices daisy-chain over an isolated serial bus to cover a 192-cell pack from a single controller. Each device also reports cell temperature from two dedicated thermistor inputs, sampled at 10 Hz during normal operation. Anvil's qualification testing recorded voltage-measurement drift of under 150 microvolts across a 10-year simulated service life at 45 degrees Celsius, using accelerated aging equivalent to roughly 3,000 full charge cycles.

title: Passive Cell Balancing
text: Cascade's baseline balancing scheme is passive: whenever the pack controller detects a cell sitting above the pack's average state of charge by more than a configured threshold, it switches a small bleed resistor across that cell to discharge it slightly faster than its neighbors during charging. This approach is simple and inexpensive, since it requires only a resistor and a switch per cell channel, already present on every CVM-16 device, but it wastes the excess energy as heat rather than recovering it. Passive balancing runs only while the pack is charging or resting near full charge, since bleeding a cell during active discharge would worsen rather than correct the imbalance the pack is trying to equalize. Anvil's field data shows passive balancing alone is sufficient for the great majority of deployed packs, whose cells age closely enough together that large imbalances rarely develop.

title: Passive Balancing Specifications
text: Each CVM-16 balancing channel switches a 100-ohm bleed resistor across its cell, drawing approximately 40 milliamps at a typical 4.0-volt cell voltage, dissipating roughly 160 milliwatts per actively balancing cell. Correcting a 50-millivolt imbalance across a fully populated 192-cell pack under passive balancing alone takes approximately 3.1 hours under typical overnight charging conditions. Anvil's field telemetry across roughly 40,000 deployed packs shows a median maximum cell-voltage spread of 12 millivolts at end of charge, with fewer than 2 percent of packs ever triggering passive balancing on more than 8 cells simultaneously in a single charge cycle.

title: Balancing Mode Comparison
text:
Passive and active balancing trade off cost, efficiency, and speed differently across Cascade's two supported balancing modes, summarized below.

| Balancing mode | Balancing current | Energy efficiency | Time to correct 50 mV spread |
|---|---|---|---|
| None (unbalanced reference) | 0 mA | n/a | never converges |
| Passive (bleed) | 40 mA | 0% recovered | 3.1 hours |
| Active (charge-shuttling) | 300 mA | 85% recovered | 0.5 hours |

Active balancing modules are offered only on Cascade's higher pack-capacity configurations, since the added inductor-based shuttling hardware adds cost that is harder to justify on smaller, lower-value packs.

title: Active Balancing and State-of-Charge Estimation
text: Cascade's optional active-balancing module replaces the bleed resistor's wasted heat with an inductor-based charge shuttle that moves energy directly from a high-voltage cell to its lower-voltage neighbor rather than discharging it to nothing. State-of-charge estimation runs independently of balancing, combining coulomb counting, which integrates measured current over time, with periodic correction from an open-circuit-voltage curve whenever the pack rests long enough for cell voltages to settle. Coulomb counting alone drifts over time because small current-sensor offset errors accumulate, so Cascade fuses the two signals using an extended Kalman filter that weights the voltage-based correction more heavily whenever the pack has been at rest, and weights the coulomb-counting integral more heavily during active driving when voltage alone is a poor state-of-charge indicator.

title: State-of-Charge Accuracy
text: Cascade's fused state-of-charge estimate holds root-mean-square error under 2.1 percent across a full charge-discharge cycle, validated against a reference laboratory coulometer on 24 pack samples drawn from three vehicle platforms. Coulomb counting alone, without Kalman correction, drifts to a mean absolute error of 6.8 percent by the end of a simulated 400-kilometer driving cycle. The extended Kalman filter recalibrates against the open-circuit-voltage curve during any rest period exceeding 20 minutes, which field telemetry shows occurs at least once daily for roughly 91 percent of deployed vehicles. Current-sensor offset, the primary source of coulomb-counting drift, measures a mean of 18 milliamps across Cascade's shunt-based current sensors.

title: Kalman Filter Formulation
text: Cascade's SoC estimator implements a standard discrete-time extended Kalman filter with state x_k representing state of charge, following the prediction step x_k = x_(k-1) - (I_k * dt) / Q_rated + w_k, where I_k is measured current, dt is the sample interval, Q_rated is the cell's rated capacity, and w_k is process noise. The correction step compares predicted open-circuit voltage against measured terminal voltage, updating the state estimate weighted by the Kalman gain K_k = P_k * H^T * (H * P_k * H^T + R)^-1, where P_k is the estimate covariance, H is the voltage-to-SoC measurement Jacobian, and R is measurement noise variance. Cascade tunes R higher during active driving, when terminal voltage is a noisier SoC indicator due to load-dependent internal resistance drop, and lower at rest, letting the filter trust voltage corrections more heavily exactly when they are most reliable.

title: Factory Calibration Procedure
text:
Technicians commissioning a new Cascade pack in the factory follow this calibration sequence before the pack is approved for vehicle installation. 1. Charge every cell to a known reference voltage using external bench chargers rather than the pack's own balancing hardware.
2. Record each CVM-16 channel's reported voltage against the bench reference and compute a per-channel offset correction.
3. Write the offset corrections into the pack controller's non-volatile calibration table.
4. Run a full passive-balancing cycle and confirm resulting cell-voltage spread falls under 5 millivolts.
5. Log the calibration results, including all per-channel offsets, to Anvil's factory quality database before final pack sealing. A pack failing step four is re-inspected for a defective CVM-16 channel before recalibration is attempted a second time.

title: Prior Research: Kalman Fusion for SoC
text: Fusing coulomb counting with voltage-based correction is a well-established technique outside Anvil's own engineering literature. Zhang and Liu's 2017 paper, 基于扩展卡尔曼滤波的锂离子电池荷电状态估计方法研究 (Research on Lithium-Ion Battery State-of-Charge Estimation Using Extended Kalman Filtering), evaluated filter convergence speed under varying initial-condition errors, a concern directly relevant to Cascade's cold-start behavior when a pack is first powered after extended storage. Their finding that convergence time scales roughly with the square of the initial SoC estimate error, rather than linearly, informed Anvil's decision to seed Cascade's filter with a conservative mid-range SoC guess at cold start rather than assuming a full charge, reducing worst-case convergence time substantially in the field.

title: Thermal-Runaway Detection
text: Cascade's thermal-runaway detection layer monitors each cell's temperature-rise rate rather than absolute temperature alone, since absolute temperature varies too much with ambient conditions and charging rate to serve as a reliable standalone trigger. A sustained rise rate above a calibrated threshold, held for longer than a short debounce window, triggers an immediate pack-level contactor opening, electrically isolating the pack before a single cell's failure can propagate thermally to its neighbors. Cascade's higher-end pack configurations pair this thermal-rate monitoring with an off-gas sensor that detects the specific volatile compounds a lithium-ion cell releases in the earliest stage of thermal runaway, often before any measurable temperature rise occurs at all. Combining both signals catches failure modes that either signal alone would miss, at the cost of additional sensor hardware and calibration complexity.

title: Detection Thresholds and Timing
text: Cascade's default thermal-runaway trigger fires when any cell's temperature rise rate exceeds 4 degrees Celsius per minute, sustained for at least 3 seconds to filter out brief sensor noise spikes. Contactor opening completes within 45 milliseconds of trigger confirmation, verified during Anvil's qualification testing across 200 induced-fault trials using a resistive heating element to simulate internal cell failure. Packs equipped with the optional off-gas sensor detected simulated early-stage runaway an average of 94 seconds before the temperature-rate threshold alone would have triggered, based on the same 200-trial qualification set. False-positive trigger rate in field deployment across roughly 40,000 packs has remained below 0.003 percent annually since the detection threshold was last recalibrated in 2022.

title: Detection Hardware by Generation
text:
Thermal-runaway detection response time has improved measurably across Cascade's three hardware generations, as the qualification data below shows.

| Generation | Sensor type | Detection latency | False-positive rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gen 1 (2018) | Temperature-rate only | 210 ms | 0.02%/yr |
| Gen 2 (2020) | Temperature-rate + coarse gas | 95 ms | 0.008%/yr |
| Gen 3 (2023) | Temperature-rate + calibrated gas | 45 ms | 0.003%/yr |

Each generation's false-positive-rate improvement came primarily from better gas-sensor calibration against ambient off-gassing sources unrelated to actual cell failure, such as adhesives and battery-pack coatings.

title: Propagation-Delay Safety Margin
text: Cascade's thermal team models inter-cell runaway propagation delay as t_prop = k * d^2 / alpha, a simplified thermal-diffusion approximation where d is the physical spacing between adjacent cells, alpha is the pack's effective thermal diffusivity between cells, and k is an empirically fitted constant capturing the pack's specific thermal-barrier material. Anvil's qualification data fits k such that t_prop stays above 90 seconds for Cascade's standard 3-millimeter inter-cell barrier spacing, comfortably exceeding the 45-millisecond contactor-opening latency described elsewhere by more than three orders of magnitude. This large margin is deliberate: since t_prop scales with the square of spacing, even manufacturing variance that reduces effective spacing by 20 percent still leaves propagation delay far above detection-and-response latency, meaning contactor opening reliably isolates a failing cell before thermal propagation reaches its neighbors under any spacing tolerance Anvil's manufacturing process actually produces.

title: Prior Research: Off-Gas Detection
text: Off-gas-based early detection research has a substantial French-language literature that predates most English-language automotive-industry publications on the topic. Fournier's 2019 study, Détection précoce de l'emballement thermique par analyse des gaz de dégazage dans les cellules lithium-ion (Early Detection of Thermal Runaway Through Off-Gas Analysis in Lithium-Ion Cells), identified the specific volatile-compound signature Cascade's Gen 3 gas sensor is calibrated against, roughly 94 seconds ahead of any measurable temperature-rate threshold. Anvil's thermal engineering team cites Fournier's compound-signature work directly in the Gen 3 sensor's calibration documentation, noting that the French study's laboratory-scale detection lead time closely matched Anvil's own field qualification results despite substantial differences in cell chemistry and form factor between the two testing programs.

title: FAQ: CVM-16 Voltage Resolution
text: Q: What voltage resolution does the CVM-16 monitoring IC provide? A: Cascade's cell-voltage monitoring IC, the CVM-16, monitors up to 16 series-connected cells per device and reports each cell's voltage through a 16-bit delta-sigma ADC with 22-microvolt resolution. This resolution is more than sufficient to detect the kind of slow, gradual cell-voltage drift that precedes most degradation-related pack failures, well before the drift becomes large enough to affect balancing or range estimation. Anvil does not currently offer a higher-resolution monitoring IC variant, since field data has not shown any degradation mode that existing resolution fails to catch with adequate lead time.

title: Changelog: Firmware v5.3
text: Cascade firmware v5.3 changelog, June 2023. Adjusts the extended Kalman filter's process-noise tuning for packs operating in extended cold-climate storage. No change to the underlying filter structure. For reference, Cascade's fused state-of-charge estimate holds root-mean-square error under 3.4 percent across a full charge-discharge cycle, validated against a reference laboratory coulometer on 24 pack samples drawn from three vehicle platforms, consistent with the accuracy figures published for prior firmware versions. This release also fixes a rare rounding error in the rest-period detection logic that occasionally delayed open-circuit-voltage recalibration by up to two minutes on packs with a noisy current sensor near the rest threshold.

title: Corrigendum: Gen 3 Thermal Specification
text: Corrigendum to Cascade Gen 3 Thermal Detection Specification, issued October 2023. The originally published specification contained an error in the stated default trigger threshold. Cascade's default thermal-runaway trigger fires when any cell's temperature rise rate exceeds 6 degrees Celsius per minute, sustained for at least 3 seconds to filter out brief sensor noise spikes. The originally published 4-degree figure was a draft value used during early Gen 2 development that was never updated when Gen 3 shipped with a revised threshold following additional false-positive testing. Deployed Gen 3 packs were unaffected by this documentation error, since firmware always used the corrected 6-degree threshold; only the written specification was wrong.

title: History: From Pack-Level Monitoring to Cell-Level Intelligence
text: Anvil Energy began developing what would become Cascade in 2016, initially as a pack-level monitoring system with no cell-level visibility at all, measuring only total pack voltage and current the way most early electric-vehicle battery systems did at the time. That first-generation design shipped to a single small delivery-van customer in 2017 and quickly ran into a problem common to pack-level-only monitoring: a single degrading cell could pull the entire pack's usable capacity down long before any pack-level measurement gave an operator warning that something was wrong. The team's response was to redesign around per-cell monitoring, leading directly to the first CVM-16 device and the Cascade name, launched commercially in 2018. Early CVM-16 deployments used only passive balancing, since active balancing hardware was judged too expensive and too immature for the automotive-grade reliability Anvil's early customers required. Thermal-runaway detection was not part of the original 2018 design at all; it was added as a dedicated feature only after a 2019 industry-wide series of well-publicized thermal-runaway incidents at other manufacturers made cell-level thermal monitoring a customer requirement rather than an optional differentiator. That first thermal-detection generation, temperature-rate monitoring alone, shipped in what the team retroactively calls Gen 1, followed within two years by a coarse gas-sensing addition in Gen 2 once off-gas detection research matured enough to be practical in a production automotive part. State-of-charge estimation went through its own separate evolution: the original coulomb-counting-only approach drifted noticeably over long trips, generating enough customer complaints about inaccurate range estimates that Anvil's software team spent most of 2020 developing the Kalman-filter fusion approach described in this specification's estimation sections, drawing heavily on published academic work rather than starting from scratch. Active balancing arrived last, in 2021, offered initially only as an expensive option for high-capacity commercial packs, once the inductor-based shuttling hardware's cost had fallen enough to make it commercially viable for at least some of Anvil's product line. Looking back, Anvil's engineering leadership describes the shift from pack-level to cell-level monitoring as the single most consequential architectural decision in Cascade's history, since every later feature, balancing, thermal detection, and refined state-of-charge estimation, depends on the cell-level visibility that first redesign established. They also note that thermal-runaway detection, unlike most of Cascade's other features, was driven primarily by external industry events rather than by Anvil's own internal roadmap, a pattern the team has since tried to anticipate more proactively for future safety-relevant features.

title: Incident Postmortem: The November 2021 Contained Cell Failure
text: In November 2021, a Cascade Gen 2 pack installed in a commercial delivery van experienced an internal cell short circuit while parked overnight at a depot in a fleet operator's charging yard, providing the first real-world validation of Cascade's thermal-runaway detection under an actual, rather than simulated, fault condition. The affected cell developed an internal short from a manufacturing defect traced later to a folded separator layer introduced during the cell vendor's electrode-winding process, a defect type the cell vendor's own incoming inspection had not been designed to catch. Cascade's coarse gas sensor detected an off-gas signature consistent with early-stage thermal runaway approximately 70 seconds before the temperature-rate threshold would have triggered on its own, opening the pack contactor and electrically isolating the affected cell string well before the cell's temperature rise became severe. The van's onboard telematics system simultaneously alerted the fleet operator's overnight monitoring service, which dispatched an inspection crew within twenty minutes, though by the time the crew arrived the affected cell had already cooled without any external fire or visible damage to neighboring cells. Post-incident teardown confirmed the failure was contained entirely within the single affected cell; no thermal propagation to adjacent cells occurred, consistent with the wide safety margin Cascade's propagation-delay modeling predicts for its standard inter-cell barrier spacing. Anvil's investigation identified two contributing factors beyond the cell defect itself: first, the fleet operator had been charging the affected van at a higher-than-recommended current for its ambient temperature, which likely accelerated the defective cell's failure timeline, though it was not judged the root cause. Second, Cascade's gas-sensor calibration at the time used a fixed detection threshold that did not adjust for ambient humidity, and post-incident analysis showed humidity-adjusted calibration would likely have detected the off-gas signature roughly 15 seconds earlier still. Anvil's remediation shipped in a Gen 2 firmware update within eight weeks, adding humidity compensation to the gas-sensor calibration curve, and separately recommended the fleet operator adjust its charging-current profile for cold-weather depot conditions. The cell vendor implemented additional electrode-winding inspection specifically targeting folded-separator defects, and Anvil began requiring evidence of that inspection step as a condition of continued cell supply for Cascade packs. The incident report concluded that the detection system performed as designed and that the eventual outcome, a contained single-cell failure with no propagation and no fire, validated the core thermal-detection architecture even though it also surfaced a specific, fixable calibration gap. Anvil now includes this incident, in anonymized form, in customer-facing safety documentation as a concrete example of contained failure rather than relying solely on laboratory qualification data to make the case for Cascade's detection approach.
